1:
On 1 Corinthians 3:1 — “And I, brethren, could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal, even as unto babes in Christ” — the witnesses mark Paul’s diagnosis of the Corinthians’ immaturity.

The Eastern fathers set the argument. Chrysostom: having overturned worldly philosophy, Paul “comes unto another argument,” meeting their conceit. The grammatical-historical commentators define the terms. Robertson: “carnal” (sarkinois) here means “men o’ flesh,” the material of flesh, distinct from the worse sarkikos of verse 3. Wesley: babes “still weak in grace, though eminent in gifts.” The Puritan Henry: “those who are sanctified are so only in part; there is still room for growth” (2 Peter 3:18).

The verse opens Paul’s gentle but candid rebuke. He could not address the Corinthians as mature, spiritual believers, but only as carnal — babes in Christ. For all their boasted wisdom and gifts, they were spiritually infants, undeveloped and weak.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the Corinthians’ self-conceit with the truth of their immaturity. The witnesses note the tender address — “brethren… babes in Christ” — which softens the rebuke even as it lands: they are truly in Christ, yet only infants. They mark the irony Henry presses: a church proud of its wisdom is told it is spiritually childish. The carnality named here is not the unregenerate state but the lingering dominance of the flesh in believers, which kept them from the deeper things of the gospel. The verse thus sets the theme of the chapter: the Corinthians’ factions and pride betray not maturity but its opposite, a failure to grow up into Christ. Paul, the wise teacher, suits his words to their condition, addressing them as the babes they had shown themselves to be — and so begins to lead them, by rebuke and instruction, toward the spiritual maturity their divisions denied.

So the witnesses see Paul confront the Corinthians’ self-conceit with the truth of their immaturity. The tender address — “brethren… babes in Christ” — softens the rebuke even as it lands: they are truly in Christ, yet only infants. The carnality named here is not the unregenerate state but the lingering dominance of the flesh in believers. A church proud of its wisdom is told it is spiritually childish — and Paul, the wise teacher, suits his words to their condition, beginning to lead them by rebuke and instruction toward the maturity their divisions denied.
2:
On 1 Corinthians 3:2 — “I have fed you with milk, and not with meat: for hitherto ye were not able to bear it, neither yet now are ye able” — the witnesses mark the milk and meat of doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the figure. Barnes: “milk” denotes “the more simple and elementary doctrines… the new birth, repentance, faith” (Hebrews 5:12–14). Robertson notes “I fed you with milk” (gala…epotisa), the causative verb. Wesley: “the first and plainest truths… so should every preacher suit his doctrine to his hearers.” The Reformed Calvin clarifies that this refers “to the manner and form… rather than to the substance of the doctrine” — the same gospel, suited to capacity.

The verse continues the metaphor of infancy. Paul fed the Corinthians milk, the elementary truths, because they could not yet digest solid food — and, he adds pointedly, they still cannot. Their immaturity, far from passing, persists.

So the witnesses see Paul both explain his method and rebuke their stagnation. The witnesses note the wisdom of suiting the teaching to the hearer: as a nurse gives an infant milk, so Paul gave the young converts the simplest doctrines, not the deeper mysteries they could not bear. They mark Calvin’s important distinction — the difference is in the manner, not the substance; the same Christ is preached to babes and to the mature, but adapted to their capacity. Yet the sting lies in “neither yet now are ye able”: years on, the Corinthians remain infants, still unable to receive solid food. The verse thus rebukes a church that should have grown but had not, content with milk when they ought to have advanced to meat. It teaches both the pastoral wisdom of meeting people where they are and the shame of arrested growth — a people whose persisting carnality, shown in their divisions, has kept them babes when they should be mature.

So the witnesses see Paul both explain his method and rebuke their stagnation. As a nurse gives an infant milk, so he gave the young converts the simplest doctrines, not the deeper mysteries they could not bear. They mark Calvin’s distinction — the difference is in the manner, not the substance; the same Christ is preached, adapted to capacity. Yet the sting lies in “neither yet now are ye able”: years on, the Corinthians remain infants. The verse teaches both the pastoral wisdom of meeting people where they are and the shame of arrested growth.
3:
On 1 Corinthians 3:3 — “For ye are yet carnal: for whereas there is among you envying, and strife, and divisions, are ye not carnal, and walk as men?” — the witnesses mark the proof of their carnality.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word and the evidence. Robertson: “carnal” here is sarkikos, “fitted for the flesh,” living “according to the flesh.” Clarke lists the three faults — envying (the soul), strife (the words), divisions (the deeds), “wrong in thought, word, and deed.” Ellicott: “after the manner of man,” not spiritually enlightened. The Reformed Calvin: while the flesh prevails, “the wisdom of God finds no admittance.”

The verse names the proof of their immaturity: their quarrels. Envy, strife, and divisions among them show that they are still carnal, walking like mere men of the world rather than as spiritual Christians. Their conduct convicts them.

So the witnesses see Paul point to the Corinthians’ factions as the undeniable evidence of their carnal state. The witnesses note the progression Clarke marks — envy in the heart, strife in the speech, divisions in the body — a single carnal disposition working out in thought, word, and deed. They observe that the test of spirituality is not gifts or knowledge, which the Corinthians had in abundance, but the temper of the life; and their jealous contention betrayed that the flesh still ruled. To “walk as men” is to live by the principles of the unconverted world, governed by rivalry and party-spirit rather than by the Spirit of Christ. The verse thus exposes the root of the Corinthian problem: their divisions were not a small fault but the symptom of a deep spiritual immaturity. A church torn by envy and strife, however gifted, is a carnal church — and Paul will show them the better way by setting the true place of ministers in its proper light.

So the witnesses see Paul point to the Corinthians’ factions as the undeniable evidence of their carnal state. They note the progression Clarke marks — envy in the heart, strife in the speech, divisions in the body. The test of spirituality is not gifts or knowledge, which they had in abundance, but the temper of the life; and their jealous contention betrayed that the flesh still ruled. A church torn by envy and strife, however gifted, is a carnal church — and Paul will show them the better way by setting the true place of ministers in its proper light.
4:
On 1 Corinthians 3:4 — “For while one saith, I am of Paul; and another, I am of Apollos; are ye not carnal?” — the witnesses mark the slogans of party-spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the factions (1 Corinthians 1:12). Clarke: Paul and Apollos “held the same creed… not the slightest difference,” so to prefer one was “the fullest proof of their carnality.” Wesley: Paul names himself and Apollos “to show that he would condemn any division… even… in favour of himself” — “the Spirit of God allows no party zeal.” Calvin: each “gloried in his particular master” (Matthew 23:8).

The verse gives the very words of the Corinthian factions. To say “I am of Paul” or “I am of Apollos” is to act carnally, dividing the church by attachment to human teachers. The slogans themselves are the symptom.

So the witnesses see Paul name the party-cries that proved the Corinthians’ carnality. The witnesses mark the telling point that Paul and Apollos taught the same gospel; to set one above the other was therefore not a difference of doctrine but a mere clannish preference of persons — the essence of carnal division. They note Wesley’s observation that Paul condemns the faction even where it flattered himself: the Spirit allows no party zeal, even on behalf of the truest teacher. To say “I am of Paul” was to forget that Paul was but a servant, and to give to a man the loyalty due to Christ alone. The verse thus lays bare the folly at the heart of the schism: the Corinthians had turned the gifts of Christ’s ministers into the banners of rival sects. Paul will now correct this by showing what ministers really are — not heads of parties, but servants through whom God works.

So the witnesses see Paul name the party-cries that proved the Corinthians’ carnality. They mark the telling point that Paul and Apollos taught the same gospel; to set one above the other was not a difference of doctrine but a clannish preference of persons. They note Wesley’s observation that Paul condemns the faction even where it flattered himself: the Spirit allows no party zeal, even for the truest teacher. To say “I am of Paul” was to forget that Paul was but a servant, and to give to a man the loyalty due to Christ alone — a folly Paul will now correct.
5:
On 1 Corinthians 3:5 — “Who then is Paul, and who is Apollos, but ministers by whom ye believed, even as the Lord gave to every man?” — the witnesses mark the true place of ministers.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the office. Robertson: not “who” (tis) but “what” (ti) — “ministers” (diakonoi), “merely servants through whom ye believed,” not leaders of sects. Barnes: they are “on a level… all but ministers, or servants.” The Puritan Henry: “they are but ministers… by whom you believed.” Calvin: Paul names himself and Apollos “that he may avoid any appearance of envy.”

The verse reduces the magnified teachers to their true rank. What are Paul and Apollos? Merely servants, instruments through whom the Corinthians came to faith, each doing the work the Lord assigned. They are not masters to be followed, but ministers used by God.

So the witnesses see Paul deflate the party-spirit by defining what ministers are. The witnesses note the deliberate “what,” not “who” — reducing the persons over whom the Corinthians quarrelled to mere functions, servants in God’s hand. The faith of the Corinthians came not from Paul or Apollos as its source, but through them as instruments, and according to the gifts the Lord apportioned to each. They mark that this levels the teachers: all are servants, none the head of a sect, none deserving the loyalty due to Christ. The verse thus strikes at the foundation of the Corinthian factions: to glory in a minister is to mistake a servant for a master. Paul models the humility he commends, naming himself first among the servants. He will now unfold the figure of planting and watering to show how the various ministers, far from being rivals, are fellow-labourers in one work, all dependent on the God who alone gives the increase.

So the witnesses see Paul deflate the party-spirit by defining what ministers are. The deliberate “what,” not “who,” reduces the persons over whom the Corinthians quarrelled to mere functions, servants in God’s hand. Their faith came not from Paul or Apollos as its source but through them as instruments. This levels the teachers: all are servants, none the head of a sect. Paul models the humility he commends, naming himself first — and will now unfold the figure of planting and watering to show that the various ministers are fellow-labourers in one work, all dependent on the God who gives the increase.
6:
On 1 Corinthians 3:6 — “I have planted, Apollos watered; but God gave the increase” — the witnesses mark the figure of the field.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the agriculture. Robertson: “I planted” (ephyteusa) at Corinth (Acts 18:1), “Apollos watered” (Acts 18:24); “but God gave the increase” (imperfect, kept on giving). Barnes: Paul “established the church… the first preacher.” The Reformed Calvin: after all the husbandman’s labour, the growth is God’s; the word and preaching are the means, the increase his alone.

The verse pictures the ministry as farming. Paul planted the church, Apollos watered it with further teaching — but God alone gave the growth. The ministers do their distinct tasks; the life and increase are wholly from God.

So the witnesses see in planting and watering the harmony and subordination of the ministers. The witnesses note the apt division of labour: Paul, the founder, sowed the seed at Corinth; Apollos, coming after, watered it by his teaching — complementary works, not rival ones. But the decisive word is the last: “God gave the increase.” Neither the planter nor the waterer could make the seed grow; that was God’s sole prerogative, continued (the imperfect tense suggests) throughout. They mark Calvin’s point that the means — the word and its faithful ministry — are necessary, yet powerless without the divine blessing that alone gives life. The verse thus dissolves the ground of the Corinthian factions: Paul and Apollos were not competitors but co-labourers in God’s field, each doing his part, and both nothing without God. To glory in one over the other is to forget that the increase — the only thing that matters — comes from God alone.

So the witnesses see in planting and watering the harmony and subordination of the ministers. Paul, the founder, sowed the seed; Apollos, coming after, watered it — complementary works, not rival ones. But the decisive word is the last: “God gave the increase.” Neither planter nor waterer could make the seed grow; that was God’s sole prerogative. The means — the word and its faithful ministry — are necessary, yet powerless without the divine blessing. To glory in one over the other is to forget that the increase, the only thing that matters, comes from God alone.
7:
On 1 Corinthians 3:7 — “So then neither is he that planteth any thing, neither he that watereth; but God that giveth the increase” — the witnesses mark the nothingness of the ministers.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the comparison. Barnes: “they are nothing in comparison with God… the honour is due to God.” Robertson: “God is the whole and we are not anything.” Clarke: “God alone should have all the glory.” Wesley: “without him neither planting nor watering avails.” The Reformed Calvin notes Paul depreciates the ministers not absolutely but in respect of the glory due to God.

The verse draws the conclusion: the ministers are nothing; God is everything. Neither planter nor waterer counts for anything in comparison with the God who gives the growth. All the honour belongs to him.

So the witnesses see Paul press the logic to its humbling end. The witnesses note that the “nothing” is comparative, not absolute — the ministers’ labour is real and necessary, as the next verse will affirm, but it is nothing beside the divine power that alone gives increase. Set against God, the planter and waterer vanish into insignificance; the glory cannot be shared. They mark how decisively this overthrows the Corinthian boasting: if the ministers are nothing, how absurd to form parties around them. The verse thus completes the lesson of the field: the whole efficacy of the ministry lies in God, who gives the increase; the servants are mere instruments, important in their place yet nothing in themselves. It teaches the Corinthians, and every church, to look past the instruments to the God who works through them, and to give him alone the glory — a truth that leaves no room for glorying in men.

So the witnesses see Paul press the logic to its humbling end. The “nothing” is comparative, not absolute — the ministers’ labour is real and necessary, but it is nothing beside the divine power that gives increase. Set against God, the planter and waterer vanish into insignificance; the glory cannot be shared. They mark how decisively this overthrows the Corinthian boasting: if the ministers are nothing, how absurd to form parties around them. It teaches the church to look past the instruments to the God who works through them, and to give him alone the glory.
8:
On 1 Corinthians 3:8 — “Now he that planteth and he that watereth are one: and every man shall receive his own reward according to his own labour” — the witnesses mark the unity and the reward.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both truths. Barnes: they “are one” in being “united in reference to the same work,” the same end. Ellicott: “each man shall receive his own reward… not from man… according to his labour” — “individuality as well as… unity.” Robertson: the planter and waterer “work together.” The Reformed Calvin: the Corinthians wrongly tore asunder those who “with united efforts are aiming at one and the same thing.”

The verse joins two truths: the ministers are one, yet each is individually rewarded. United in aim and work, they are not rivals; yet God will reward each according to his own faithful labour. Unity of purpose, individuality of reward.

So the witnesses see Paul balance the oneness of the ministers with their personal accountability. The witnesses note the unity: planter and waterer are “one” — not the same person, but one in cause, aim, and the work of saving souls, so that to set them against each other is to tear apart what God has joined. Yet within that unity stands individuality: each minister will receive his own reward, measured not by men’s esteem or the size of his apparent success, but by God’s reckoning of his faithful labour. They mark how this both rebukes the factions — the ministers are one, so why divide over them? — and comforts the faithful servant, whose reward rests with God, not man. The verse thus teaches that the ministry is a shared work with personal accountability: all labour together for one end, yet each answers to God for his own toil. It dignifies faithful service while denying any ground for rivalry, for the reward comes from God according to labour, not from partisans according to fame.

So the witnesses see Paul balance the oneness of the ministers with their personal accountability. Planter and waterer are “one” in cause, aim, and the work of saving souls, so that to set them against each other is to tear apart what God has joined. Yet within that unity stands individuality: each will receive his own reward, measured not by men’s esteem but by God’s reckoning of his faithful labour. This both rebukes the factions — the ministers are one — and comforts the faithful servant, whose reward rests with God, not man. The ministry is a shared work with personal accountability.
9:
On 1 Corinthians 3:9 — “For we are labourers together with God: ye are God’s husbandry, ye are God’s building” — the witnesses mark the threefold “God’s.”

The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphatic repetition. Ellicott: “thrice… the Apostle repeats the name of God… We are GOD’S fellow-labourers; you are GOD’S field—GOD’S house.” Robertson: “God’s fellow-workers” (theou synergoi) — “God is the major partner… but he lets us work with him” (2 Corinthians 6:1). Calvin reads it as “the best argument” — the reward is sure because the work is the Lord’s.

The verse sums up the figure and pivots to a new one. The ministers are God’s fellow-workers; the Corinthians are God’s field and God’s building. Everything belongs to God — the workers, the field, the house.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up the argument under the great possessive: God’s. The witnesses note the thrice-repeated name, fixing all on God: the ministers are his co-workers, dignified by partnership with him yet wholly his servants; the church is his tilled field, the soil he cultivates; and his building, the house he rears. They mark the wonder Robertson draws out — that God, who needs no help, graciously lets his servants labour with him. The verse thus exalts the ministry and humbles it at once: to be a fellow-worker with God is a high honour, yet the church is God’s, not the minister’s, and the glory is his. It also turns the figure from farming to building, preparing the next section on the foundation and the materials. The Corinthians, far from belonging to Paul or Apollos, belong to God; and their teachers are but God’s servants, working in God’s field, raising God’s house — a truth that leaves the factions without foundation.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up the argument under the great possessive: God’s. The thrice-repeated name fixes all on God: the ministers are his co-workers, the church his tilled field and his building. They mark the wonder Robertson draws out — that God, who needs no help, graciously lets his servants labour with him. The verse exalts the ministry and humbles it at once: to be a fellow-worker with God is a high honour, yet the church is God’s, and the glory is his. The Corinthians, far from belonging to Paul or Apollos, belong to God — a truth that leaves the factions without foundation.
10:
On 1 Corinthians 3:10 — “According to the grace of God which is given unto me, as a wise masterbuilder, I have laid the foundation, and another buildeth thereon. But let every man take heed how he buildeth” — the witnesses mark the figure of building.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the humility and the warning. Ellicott: Paul commences “according to the grace of God” to show “he is not indulging in self-laudation” (Romans 12:3). Clarke: the “wise master-builder” (sophos architekton) laid the foundation by God’s pattern. Robertson: Paul absolves Apollos and himself from the divisions. The Puritan Henry: “there may be bad building on a good foundation… let every man take heed.”

The verse casts Paul as the skilled builder who laid the foundation, and warns those who build after him. By God’s grace he laid Christ as the foundation; others build upon it — but each must take heed how he builds.

So the witnesses see Paul, with modest reference to God’s grace, take up the figure of the building. The witnesses note that he claims the role of the wise master-builder — the founder who laid the one foundation — yet at once ascribes the skill to God’s grace, not himself. They mark that the warning, “let every man take heed how he buildeth,” is addressed to all who teach and build the church after him: the foundation being rightly laid, the danger now lies in the superstructure. Henry’s point is apt — one may build badly on a good foundation, raising error or worthless work on Christ. The verse thus shifts the responsibility to the builders who follow, and prepares the searching test of the next verses: the foundation is Christ, fixed and sole; but the materials each builder adds will be tried by fire. Paul both defends his own faithful founding and solemnly charges every subsequent teacher to build with care, for the quality of the work will one day be revealed.

So the witnesses see Paul, with modest reference to God’s grace, take up the figure of the building. He claims the role of the wise master-builder — the founder who laid the one foundation — yet ascribes the skill to grace, not himself. The warning, “let every man take heed how he buildeth,” is addressed to all who build the church after him: the foundation being rightly laid, the danger now lies in the superstructure. Henry’s point is apt — one may build badly on a good foundation. Paul defends his faithful founding and charges every teacher to build with care, for the work will be tried by fire.
11:
On 1 Corinthians 3:11 — “For other foundation can no man lay than that is laid, which is Jesus Christ” — the witnesses mark the sole foundation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the exclusiveness. Robertson: “other foundation” (themelion allon, masculine because Paul has Christ in mind), not heteron, a different gospel (Galatians 1:6). The Puritan Henry: “upon this rock all the Christians found their hopes.” Barnes: the foundation is “the doctrine respecting Christ.” The Reformed Calvin: Christ is “the only foundation of the Church.”

The verse fixes the one foundation. No one can lay any foundation but the one already laid — Jesus Christ. He is the sole basis of the church and of every believer’s hope; no other can be substituted.

So the witnesses see Paul declare Christ the one and only foundation. The witnesses note the absolute exclusiveness: not merely that Christ is the best foundation, but that no other is possible; the foundation has been laid, once for all, and is Christ himself. They mark that all other supposed foundations — human merit, descent, ritual, philosophy — are sandy and false; the church stands on Christ alone. Calvin presses that the Corinthians, tickled with novelty, must be brought back to Christ alone. The verse thus guards the gospel at its base: whatever is built, the foundation is unalterable. The wise master-builder laid it; no later teacher may lay another. This both secures the church — resting on the immovable rock of Christ — and frames the warning to come: since the foundation is fixed and sure, the question is only what each builder raises upon it, and whether his work will stand the fire. Christ the foundation cannot be changed; the superstructure can be worthless or precious.

So the witnesses see Paul declare Christ the one and only foundation. Not merely that Christ is the best foundation, but that no other is possible; the foundation has been laid, once for all, and is Christ himself. All other supposed foundations — human merit, descent, ritual, philosophy — are sandy and false; the church stands on Christ alone. This both secures the church, resting on the immovable rock, and frames the warning to come: the foundation is fixed, but the superstructure can be worthless or precious. Christ the foundation cannot be changed; the building raised upon it will be tested.
12:
On 1 Corinthians 3:12 — “Now if any man build upon this foundation gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble” — the witnesses mark the two kinds of building materials.

The grammatical-historical commentators distinguish the materials. Robertson: three durable (gold, silver, marble) and three perishable (wood, hay, stubble), “of a palace on the one hand, of a mud hut on the other” (Lightfoot). Wesley: the first three “will bear the fire… true and solid doctrines”; the last three “will not… doctrines, ceremonies, and forms of human invention.” Clarke: gold/silver/stones mean “pure and wholesome doctrines,” wood/hay/stubble “false doctrines.”

The verse sets out the contrasting materials a builder may raise on the foundation. Upon Christ one may build with precious, fire-proof materials — or with cheap, combustible ones. The figure anticipates the testing fire.

So the witnesses see Paul classify the work built on Christ into the enduring and the worthless. The witnesses note the deliberate contrast — gold, silver, costly stones against wood, hay, stubble — the materials of a palace versus a hovel, and, in the interpretation, sound doctrine and faithful work against error and human invention. They mark that both sorts of builder rest on the true foundation; the question is not the foundation but the superstructure. The materials are chosen for their behaviour in fire: the precious stand, the perishable burn. The verse thus prepares the great test of verse 13: every teacher builds something on Christ, but what he builds — whether the true gold of the gospel or the stubble of vain doctrine — will be revealed for what it is when the fire of God’s judgment tries it. It is a solemn warning to all who teach in the church to build with materials that will endure, not with showy but combustible additions of their own devising.

So the witnesses see Paul classify the work built on Christ into the enduring and the worthless. The deliberate contrast — gold, silver, costly stones against wood, hay, stubble — sets the materials of a palace against a hovel, and, in the interpretation, sound doctrine against error and human invention. Both sorts of builder rest on the true foundation; the question is the superstructure. The materials are chosen for their behaviour in fire: the precious stand, the perishable burn. It is a solemn warning to all who teach to build with materials that will endure, not with showy but combustible additions of their own devising.
13:
On 1 Corinthians 3:13 — “Every man’s work shall be made manifest: for the day shall declare it, because it shall be revealed by fire; and the fire shall try every man’s work of what sort it is” — the witnesses mark the testing day.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the day and the fire. Robertson: “the day” is the day of judgment (1 Thessalonians 5:4); the work “revealed in fire” (2 Thessalonians 1:8). Ellicott: the day “bursting suddenly with a… blaze of fire” (Malachi 3:1–3). Barnes: it “shall reveal the secrets of all hearts.”

The verse declares the test that awaits every builder’s work. The day of judgment will make manifest what each has built, and the fire will try its quality. No work can hide; the fire will show what it truly is.

So the witnesses see Paul announce the inescapable testing of every minister’s labour. The witnesses note that “the day” is the day of the Lord, the judgment, which will bring to light what is now hidden — the true quality of every teacher’s work. The fire is the searching test of that day, trying the work as flame tries metal and timber: the gold and stones endure, the wood and stubble are consumed. They mark that the emphasis falls on the quality, “of what sort it is” — not the appearance now, but the reality then. The verse thus sets a solemn accountability over all Christian building: what is raised on Christ will be judged, not by present applause, but by God’s fire. It comforts the faithful, whose true work will stand, and warns the careless, whose showy but worthless additions will burn. The day will declare each man’s work; nothing false or flimsy will survive the fire of God’s judgment.

So the witnesses see Paul announce the inescapable testing of every minister’s labour. “The day” is the day of the Lord, which will bring to light what is now hidden — the true quality of every teacher’s work. The fire is the searching test of that day, trying the work as flame tries metal and timber. The emphasis falls on the quality, “of what sort it is” — not the appearance now, but the reality then. It comforts the faithful, whose true work will stand, and warns the careless, whose showy but worthless additions will burn. Nothing false will survive the fire of God’s judgment.
14:
On 1 Corinthians 3:14 — “If any man’s work abide which he hath built thereupon, he shall receive a reward” — the witnesses mark the reward of enduring work.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the surviving work. Robertson: “if any man’s work shall abide” (first-class condition) — “when the fire has done its work, what is left?” Barnes: if his teaching and piety prove true, “he shall receive a reward.” Wesley: “a peculiar degree of glory.” Clarke recalls the law’s purifying of vessels by fire (Numbers 31:23).

The verse gives the happy outcome for the faithful builder. If his work endures the fire — if his teaching and labour prove sound — he shall receive a reward. The work that stands the test is crowned.

So the witnesses see the bright side of the testing fire. The witnesses note that the faithful builder, who raised gold and precious stones on Christ — sound doctrine, true piety, lasting work — will find his labour surviving the flame, and will receive his reward from God. The reward, Wesley suggests, is a special degree of glory, proportioned to faithful service. They mark that this is the encouragement set against the warning: the fire that consumes the worthless preserves and approves the precious. The verse thus assures every faithful minister and worker that nothing truly built on Christ is lost; the fire only proves and reveals it, and God crowns it. It dignifies sound, enduring labour with the promise of reward, and motivates the builder to use the precious, fire-proof materials of true doctrine and genuine grace — for the work that abides will be rewarded, while, as the next verse shows, the work that burns brings loss.

So the witnesses see the bright side of the testing fire. The faithful builder, who raised gold and precious stones on Christ — sound doctrine, true piety, lasting work — will find his labour surviving the flame, and will receive his reward from God. The reward, Wesley suggests, is a special degree of glory, proportioned to faithful service. This is the encouragement set against the warning: the fire that consumes the worthless preserves and approves the precious. It dignifies sound, enduring labour with the promise of reward, and motivates the builder to use the fire-proof materials of true doctrine and genuine grace.
15:
On 1 Corinthians 3:15 — “If any man’s work shall be burned, he shall suffer loss: but he himself shall be saved; yet so as by fire” — the witnesses mark the saved-but-suffering builder.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the loss and the escape. Robertson: “shall be burned down” (katakaesetai); “he shall suffer loss” of his work, “but he himself shall be saved… so as through fire,” barely. Ellicott warns the passage is “metaphorical… forgetting this, Roman divines have evolved… the doctrine of purgatory.” Wesley: he loses “that peculiar degree of glory.”

The verse gives the outcome for the careless builder. If his work burns, he suffers loss — his labour gone — yet he himself is saved, but as one escaping through flames. The man is rescued, though his work is not.

So the witnesses see the sober yet merciful outcome for the builder whose work fails. The witnesses note the careful balance: such a man suffers real loss — his teaching and labour, found worthless, are consumed, and the reward forfeited — yet he himself, resting on the true foundation of Christ, is saved, though narrowly, “as by fire,” like one snatched from a burning house with nothing but his life. They mark Ellicott’s and Clarke’s important caution that the whole passage is metaphorical, and that the Roman doctrine of purgatory has been wrongly built on these words: the fire here is the testing fire of judgment that tries the work, not a fire that purges the soul. The verse thus warns those who build carelessly on Christ — with error or vain additions — that they will lose their reward and barely escape; and it comforts that genuine believers, however faulty their work, will not perish, being saved by their foundation. The man stands or falls by Christ; his work is tried by fire.

So the witnesses see the sober yet merciful outcome for the builder whose work fails. Such a man suffers real loss — his labour, found worthless, is consumed, and the reward forfeited — yet he himself, resting on the true foundation, is saved, though narrowly, “as by fire,” like one snatched from a burning house with nothing but his life. They mark Ellicott’s caution that the passage is metaphorical, and that the Roman doctrine of purgatory has been wrongly built on it: the fire is the testing of the work at judgment, not a purging of the soul. The man stands or falls by Christ; his work is tried by fire.
16:
On 1 Corinthians 3:16 — “Know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?” — the witnesses mark the church as God’s temple.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure’s turn. Robertson: “a sanctuary” (naos, the holy place, not hieron, the precinct) of God, the Spirit making “his home” in them. Ellicott: from grand edifices in general to “the temple of God… holy.” The Puritan Henry: “you are God’s building… the temple of God.” The Reformed Calvin turns from teachers to people: “take care that you be not… defiled.”

The verse names the church the temple of God, indwelt by his Spirit. The building Paul has spoken of is no ordinary structure but God’s holy sanctuary, where his Spirit dwells. The Corinthians are that temple.

So the witnesses see Paul lift the figure of the building to its highest dignity: the church is God’s temple. The witnesses note that the word is naos, the inner sanctuary, the holy place where God himself dwells — not a mere building but a consecrated one, made holy by the indwelling Spirit. As the Shechinah filled the temple of old, so the Spirit of God inhabits the church. They mark Calvin’s turn from the teachers, whom Paul had charged to build carefully, to the people, whom he now charges to keep the temple undefiled. The verse thus invests the church with sacred dignity and solemn obligation: it is God’s dwelling, indwelt by his Spirit, and therefore holy. This both rebukes the Corinthians’ divisions — which mar God’s temple — and prepares the dread warning of the next verse, that whoever defiles or destroys this temple God will destroy. To be the temple of God is the church’s glory; to defile it is a fearful sin.

So the witnesses see Paul lift the figure of the building to its highest dignity: the church is God’s temple. The word is naos, the inner sanctuary, the holy place where God dwells — made holy by the indwelling Spirit. As the Shechinah filled the temple of old, so the Spirit inhabits the church. They mark Calvin’s turn from the teachers, charged to build carefully, to the people, charged to keep the temple undefiled. The verse invests the church with sacred dignity and solemn obligation: it is God’s dwelling, and therefore holy — preparing the dread warning against defiling it.
17:
On 1 Corinthians 3:17 — “If any man defile the temple of God, him shall God destroy; for the temple of God is holy, which temple ye are” — the witnesses mark the warning against defiling the temple.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the matched verb. Barnes and Clarke: the Greek is the same in both clauses — “if any man destroy (phtheirei) the temple… him will God destroy,” an exact retribution, the temple being “inviolable.” Ellicott: “destroy” is the opposite of “building up.” The Reformed Calvin: a dreadful threat against those who “corrupt” the church, intruding to rule in God’s place.

The verse pronounces a fearful judgment. Whoever defiles or destroys God’s temple, God will destroy — for the temple is holy, and that temple is the church. The sin is measured by the sanctity of what is harmed.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the holiness of the church with a solemn threat. The witnesses note the exact correspondence the verb carries: he who destroys the temple, God will destroy — like for like. The sin is grave because its object is holy; to corrupt the church by false doctrine, schism, or impurity is to violate God’s sanctuary, and God will not hold the offender guiltless. They mark that this is aimed especially at the false teachers and the makers of division at Corinth, who by their factions and corrupt wisdom were marring God’s temple. The verse thus sets the destruction of the church under the severest sanction: because the temple is holy — God’s own dwelling — those who defile it incur destruction. It is a warning to every age against the sins that ruin the church, and a reminder that the church’s holiness is not a trifle but a thing God himself will vindicate, destroying those who destroy his temple.

So the witnesses see Paul guard the holiness of the church with a solemn threat. The exact correspondence the verb carries — he who destroys the temple, God will destroy — is like for like. The sin is grave because its object is holy; to corrupt the church by false doctrine, schism, or impurity is to violate God’s sanctuary. This is aimed especially at the false teachers and makers of division at Corinth, who by their factions were marring God’s temple. It is a warning to every age against the sins that ruin the church, and a reminder that its holiness is a thing God himself will vindicate.
18:
On 1 Corinthians 3:18 — “Let no man deceive himself. If any man among you seemeth to be wise in this world, let him become a fool, that he may be wise” — the witnesses mark the call to become a fool.

The Eastern fathers note the return to the theme. Chrysostom: Paul “hastens again to the battle with heathen wisdom… those who were puffed up.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “let no man deceive himself” implies some were doing so; “thinketh that he is wise.” The Puritan Henry: humility “for the remedy of the irregularities.” The Reformed Calvin: “here he puts his finger upon the true sore… they were wise in their own conceit.”

The verse calls the self-wise to a saving folly. Let no one deceive himself: the man who seems wise by worldly standards must become a fool — renounce that wisdom — to be truly wise. The path to real wisdom runs through the humbling of the false.

So the witnesses see Paul prescribe humility as the cure for the Corinthians’ conceit. The witnesses note Calvin’s point that this touches the true sore: the whole trouble sprang from their being “wise in their own conceit,” leaning on worldly wisdom and eloquence. The remedy is paradoxical — to become a fool, that is, to renounce the proud wisdom of the world and embrace the gospel the world counts foolish, and so to gain the wisdom of God. They mark the warning against self-deception: men easily flatter themselves with their own cleverness. The verse thus presses the great Corinthian lesson home to the individual: true wisdom begins where the pretensions of human wisdom end. The proud must be humbled, the self-styled wise must become fools in the world’s eyes, that they may receive the wisdom of the cross. It is the personal application of the whole argument, and the antidote to the pride that fed the factions.

So the witnesses see Paul prescribe humility as the cure for the Corinthians’ conceit. They note Calvin’s point that this touches the true sore: the whole trouble sprang from their being wise in their own conceit. The remedy is paradoxical — to become a fool, that is, to renounce the proud wisdom of the world and embrace the gospel the world counts foolish, and so to gain the wisdom of God. The warning against self-deception is pointed: men easily flatter themselves with their own cleverness. True wisdom begins where the pretensions of human wisdom end — the antidote to the pride that fed the factions.
19:
On 1 Corinthians 3:19 — “For the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God. For it is written, He taketh the wise in their own craftiness” — the witnesses mark the verdict on worldly wisdom.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the citation. Robertson: “foolishness with God” — Paul quotes Job 5:13, “he taketh” (ho drassomenos, grasps with the hand). Barnes clarifies it does not mean true science is folly, but the world’s wisdom “on the subject of religion.” Wesley: God takes the wise “by their very wisdom, which itself is their snare.”

The verse delivers God’s estimate of human wisdom. The wisdom of this world is foolishness in God’s sight; and Scripture testifies that he catches the clever in their own cunning. Their boasted wisdom becomes their trap.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm from Scripture that worldly wisdom is folly before God. The witnesses are careful, with Barnes, to note the scope: not that knowledge, science, or true learning is foolishness, but that the wisdom on which the world relies for religion and salvation is vanity in God’s sight. They mark the citation from Job — God “taketh the wise in their own craftiness” — which turns the schemer’s cleverness against himself: the very wisdom men trust becomes the snare that traps them. Wesley sharpens it: God catches them by their wisdom, which is the occasion of their ruin. The verse thus undergirds the call to become a fool: since the world’s wisdom is folly with God, and even ensnares its possessors, there is no ground for the Corinthians’ pride in it. To cling to worldly wisdom is to be caught in one’s own craft; to renounce it for the wisdom of God is the only safety. Scripture itself condemns the conceit that divided the Corinthian church.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm from Scripture that worldly wisdom is folly before God. They are careful, with Barnes, to note the scope: not that knowledge or true learning is foolishness, but that the wisdom on which the world relies for religion is vanity in God’s sight. The citation from Job — God “taketh the wise in their own craftiness” — turns the schemer’s cleverness against himself. To cling to worldly wisdom is to be caught in one’s own craft; to renounce it for the wisdom of God is the only safety. Scripture itself condemns the conceit that divided the church.
20:
On 1 Corinthians 3:20 — “And again, The Lord knoweth the thoughts of the wise, that they are vain” — the witnesses mark the second proof.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the added citation. Robertson: “another confirmatory passage from Psalm 94:11,” Paul changing “men” to “wise” for the context; “reasonings” (dialogismous), “vain” (useless, futile). Barnes: the thought is that “the thoughts of man are vain,” their schemes empty. Gill notes the rabbinic custom of a second proof-text.

The verse adds a second scriptural witness. The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, and knows them to be vain. Their reasonings, for all their show, are empty and futile in his sight.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm his verdict by a second Scripture. The witnesses note the rabbinic manner of strengthening a point with a further proof-text: to Job he adds the Psalm, that God knows the “thoughts” or reasonings of the wise to be vain. The word “vain” means useless, futile — their elaborate schemes and speculations come to nothing before God, who sees through them. They mark that Paul, applying the Psalm’s “men” to “the wise,” presses the point precisely against those who gloried in their wisdom. The verse thus piles testimony upon testimony: God not only counts worldly wisdom foolish and ensnaring, but knows its very reasonings to be empty. The combined witness of Job and the Psalms leaves the boasted wisdom of the Corinthians without defence. Their thoughts, however clever, are vain before the Lord who reads them — and the conclusion follows in the next verse: let no man therefore glory in men.

So the witnesses see Paul confirm his verdict by a second Scripture. They note the rabbinic manner of strengthening a point with a further proof-text: to Job he adds the Psalm, that God knows the reasonings of the wise to be vain — useless, futile, coming to nothing before him who sees through them. Paul, applying the Psalm’s “men” to “the wise,” presses the point against those who gloried in wisdom. The combined witness of Job and the Psalms leaves the boasted wisdom of the Corinthians without defence — and the conclusion follows: let no man therefore glory in men.
21:
On 1 Corinthians 3:21 — “Therefore let no man glory in men. For all things are yours” — the witnesses mark the conclusion against glorying in men.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the inference. Robertson: “let no one glory in men” (kauchastho), the conclusion from the self-conceit condemned. Ellicott: “let party-spirit cease… everything is yours.” The Puritan Henry: do not “pay that deference… due only to God.” The Reformed Calvin: God “takes away from mankind universally every ground of glorying.”

The verse states the practical conclusion and its surprising ground. Let no one glory in men — in human teachers — for all things are yours. The Corinthians degraded themselves by their party-cries; in truth they possessed all.

So the witnesses see Paul turn the whole argument into a liberating conclusion. The witnesses note the inference: since the ministers are mere servants and worldly wisdom is vain, there is no ground to glory in men or to enroll under their banners. But Paul does not merely forbid the factions; he overturns their very premise with a startling generosity — “all things are yours.” The Corinthians, who shrank themselves by saying “I am of Paul” or “of Apollos,” had in fact a share in all the teachers and all things. They mark Ellicott’s point that to call oneself by a man’s name is to degrade oneself, claiming a part when one owns the whole. The verse thus exposes the folly of the factions as self-impoverishment: to glory in one teacher is to forfeit the riches that belong to every believer in Christ. Paul will now spell out the breadth of that possession — teachers, world, life, death, things present and to come — all theirs, because they are Christ’s.

So the witnesses see Paul turn the whole argument into a liberating conclusion. Since the ministers are mere servants and worldly wisdom is vain, there is no ground to glory in men. But Paul does not merely forbid the factions; he overturns their premise with a startling generosity — “all things are yours.” The Corinthians, who shrank themselves by saying “I am of Paul,” had in fact a share in all. To call oneself by a man’s name is to degrade oneself, claiming a part when one owns the whole. The factions are exposed as self-impoverishment — and Paul will now spell out the breadth of the believer’s possession.
22:
On 1 Corinthians 3:22 — “Whether Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or the world, or life, or death, or things present, or things to come; all are yours” — the witnesses mark the believer’s vast inheritance.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the catalogue. Robertson: the words are “definite” though anarthrous; “all the words… belong to you.” Wesley: “the leap from Peter to the world greatly enlarges the thought” — Peter and “every one in the whole world… are also yours.” Barnes: “whatever advantages result from… any preacher… are yours.” Gill: the ministers’ gifts are “the church’s.”

The verse spreads out the believer’s possession in a sweeping list. Paul, Apollos, Cephas — all the teachers; the world, life, death, things present and to come — all belong to the Christian. Nothing is excluded.

So the witnesses marvel at the breadth of what Paul ascribes to the believer. The witnesses note the comprehensive sweep: the very teachers over whom the Corinthians quarrelled are theirs, all of them, not to be divided into rival parties but enjoyed in common; and beyond the teachers, the whole world, life and death themselves, the present and the future — all serve and belong to the people of God. They mark Wesley’s observation of the bold leap from Peter to the world, enlarging the thought to the utmost. The point against the factions is plain: why glory in one teacher when you possess them all, and infinitely more? The verse thus turns the Corinthians’ self-impoverishing party-spirit into a vision of immense riches: in Christ, all things — ministers, world, life, death, time, eternity — are theirs, working together for their good. To claim a part by saying “I am of Paul” is to forget the all that belongs to every believer, who is heir of all things in Christ.

So the witnesses marvel at the breadth of what Paul ascribes to the believer. The very teachers over whom the Corinthians quarrelled are theirs, all of them, to be enjoyed in common; and beyond the teachers, the whole world, life and death, the present and the future — all serve and belong to the people of God. They mark Wesley’s observation of the bold leap from Peter to the world. Why glory in one teacher when you possess them all, and infinitely more? The verse turns the Corinthians’ self-impoverishing party-spirit into a vision of immense riches: in Christ, all things are theirs, the believer heir of all.
23:
On 1 Corinthians 3:23 — “And ye are Christ’s; and Christ is God’s” — the witnesses mark the great chain of belonging.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the climax. Ellicott: “Christ is God’s” was added as “the great climax… avoiding any danger of the party… arrogating” the name of Christ. Barnes: “you belong to him… his property, his people.” The Reformed Calvin: Christ’s subjection “relates to… his humanity” (Philippians 2:7–8), that through him we cleave to God.

The verse completes the ascending order. All things are the believers’; the believers are Christ’s; and Christ is God’s. The chain runs up from all things, through the Christian, through Christ, to God himself.

So the witnesses see the argument crowned by the great chain of belonging. The witnesses note the ascending sequence: all things belong to the Corinthians, but the Corinthians are not their own — they belong to Christ; and Christ, in his mediatorial office, belongs to God. The riches the believer possesses are held not in independence but in subordination: all things are ours because we are Christ’s, and Christ leads us up to God. They mark Ellicott’s point that “Christ is God’s” guards against the party that took Christ’s name as a faction; even the name of Christ is not to be made a banner of division. Calvin notes that Christ’s being “God’s” concerns his humanity and mediation, by which he joins us to the Father. The verse thus sets the whole in its true frame: the believer’s vast inheritance flows from union with Christ, and Christ refers all to the glory of God. It is the answer to all glorying in men — for we are Christ’s, and Christ is God’s.

So the witnesses see the argument crowned by the great chain of belonging. All things belong to the Corinthians, but the Corinthians belong to Christ; and Christ, in his mediatorial office, belongs to God. The riches the believer possesses are held not in independence but in subordination. They mark Ellicott’s point that “Christ is God’s” guards against the party that took Christ’s name as a faction. Calvin notes that Christ’s being “God’s” concerns his humanity and mediation, by which he joins us to the Father. It is the answer to all glorying in men — for we are Christ’s, and Christ is God’s.
